Just in: Overseas Research and Selection Daily 0721: Goldman Sachs: WAIC’s embodied intelligence has surged in popularity and industrial scenarios are leading the way in commercialization.

ING: The UK’s autumn budget is expected to remain restrained and an early election may become the biggest risk to the market ING pointed out that after the new British Prime Minister Andy Burnham took office, the British bond market responded mutedly.

Although it advocates expanding investment in social housing and promoting nationalization, it has promised to abide by fiscal rules and not increase major taxes. The market is generally expected to have a relatively moderate budget this autumn.

Under the baseline scenario, the government may reduce bus fares and tax burdens on the hotel and catering industry, and transfer part of the energy policy costs from electricity charges to general taxes, in order to coordinate with the reduction of the energy price ceiling in October and suppress overall inflation.

However, the government still needs to fill a departmental spending gap of about £6 billion in 2029, as well as an unfinanced portion of the defense investment plan of £4.7 billion.

Adjustments to the fiscal framework are expected to free up an additional 16 billion pounds in borrowing space, enough to support limited increases in spending and moderate tax cuts.

Even so, UK government bond issuance is still expected to fall from 304 billion pounds in the previous fiscal year to 246 billion pounds this fiscal year, and ING expects the Bank of England to restart interest rate cuts in 2027.

The market risk is that Burnham launches a more radical plan, including expanding housing and infrastructure investment, reforming property taxes and adjusting fiscal rules.

Increasing the personal tax exemption by 10% would cost 11 billion pounds per year; replacing a 2 percentage point increase in income tax with a 2 percentage point reduction in national insurance would increase revenue by approximately 6 billion pounds.

If defense or investment spending is excluded from fiscal constraints, new debt issuance could still push gilt yields higher. ING believes that the biggest unexpected risk is an early election. The current probability given by the betting market is only 15% to 20%, but the reality may be higher.

A general election could lead to bolder borrowing plans or a policy stagnation after Labor loses its majority, making it difficult to welcome investors. Morgan Stanley: The pullback in U.S. memory chip stocks brings buying points as data center shortages continue to intensify Morgan Stanley said in its latest research report that the recent sell-off in U.S.

memory chip stocks has provided investors with attractive entry opportunities.

Although there are signs of slowing demand, rising capital expenditures, and downgrading of product configurations in some areas of the market, the shortage of data center memory continues to intensify, and this industry cycle cannot be judged according to the traditional storage cycle.

Joseph Moore, an analyst at the bank, pointed out that the core driving force of this memory boom cycle comes almost entirely from data center demand, and weak signals released by other end markets may constitute a "false alarm." Among the semiconductor targets covered by Morgan Stanley, the risk-return ratio of memory chip stocks is still lower than that of Nvidia and Broadcom, but the gap between the two is rapidly narrowing.

Morgan Stanley believes that the market has recently been worried about a second-order slowdown in memory price growth, manufacturers increasing capital expenditures, and customers controlling costs by reducing product configurations, but these changes were foreseeable a month ago.

Different from ordinary cycles, memory is increasingly becoming a key bottleneck in AI infrastructure construction and intelligent AI server CPU deployment, and the supply shortage has not yet been significantly alleviated.

According to the bank’s communication with data center purchasers, data center memory prices rose by at least 25% month-on-month in the third quarter, higher than previous forecasts by Morgan Stanley and third-party institutions. Although the increase has slowed down from the second quarter, the bank believes that this change is not unexpected.

Long-term supply agreements and reduced allocations may reduce the volatility of this price cycle and make price increases lower than previously excessive market expectations, but may prolong the duration of the industry boom. In the long term, a longer cycle duration may be more conducive to the performance of related stocks than short-term price surges.

Morgan Stanley also emphasized that market concerns about further intensification of memory shortages in 2027 and 2028 remain strong.

ING: A strong El Niño may hit at the end of the year and concerns about global food crisis are exaggerated ING said that a historically strong El Niño may occur at the end of 2026, but its impact on global agricultural supply and food prices may be lower than market concerns. The real risks are mainly concentrated in the Asia-Pacific region and some crops.

Data from the U.S.

National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration shows that sea temperature anomalies in key Pacific Ocean areas reached 1.5 degrees Celsius in June, the highest for the same period since 1982; the probability of El Niño continuing until early 2027 is 97%, and the probability of developing into a "very strong" event from October to December this year is 81%.

ING pointed out that the impact of previous moderate-intensity El Niños on the total output of major global crops has been generally limited, and food prices have not shown a steady rise.

Irrigation, drought-tolerant seeds and satellite monitoring have improved agricultural resilience: the proportion of global cultivated land equipped with irrigation facilities has increased from 11% in 1980 to 22% in 2023, and the actual proportion of irrigation has increased from 65% to nearly 83%; currently, cereal stocks are also relatively abundant, which can buffer short-term shocks.

However, the Asia-Pacific faces more significant regional risks. Australian wheat, Southeast Asian palm oil, Indian and Thai sugar, and rice production may decrease in many places. Restrictions on Peruvian anchovy fishing have pushed up fishmeal prices by more than 75% year-on-year, while feed typically accounts for 55% to 65% of aquaculture costs in Asia.

Monsoon rainfall in India is still 24% below normal, and the government has banned sugar exports until at least the end of September. Historically, Australia's wheat production fell by 58% in 2002/03 and 36% in 2023/24.

ING recommends that food and agricultural companies strengthen weather, inventory, logistics and policy monitoring, and reduce risks by increasing water and feed reserves, optimizing hedging and finding alternative raw materials.

Asia-Pacific companies may be under short-term pressure, while agricultural producers in other regions may benefit from rising prices and market share.

Goldman Sachs: If the Strait of Hormuz continues to be blocked, Brent oil prices may exceed $120 in the fourth quarter Goldman Sachs said in a research report released on July 20 that if transportation in the Strait of Hormuz continues to be blocked, Brent crude oil prices may rise to more than $120 per barrel in the fourth quarter of this year.

However, this is not the bank's baseline scenario. A team of analysts led by Daan Struyven pointed out that the escalation of tensions in the Middle East and the fact that oil shipments in the Persian Gulf have dropped to less than 45% of pre-war levels have pushed international oil prices back higher.

Goldman Sachs still assumes that tensions in the Middle East will gradually ease, and accordingly predicts that Brent crude oil prices will average US$80 per barrel in the fourth quarter and average US$75 in 2027.

However, Goldman Sachs emphasized that due to continued disruptions to shipping in the Strait of Hormuz and potential disruptions to Red Sea shipping, the risks to its oil price forecast are clearly upward.

The Red Sea route was previously an important alternative channel for transporting related goods to customers after the Persian Gulf crude oil exports were blocked. Once this route is also affected, the pressure on global oil supply may further intensify.

The research report pointed out that the decline in global crude oil inventories in the second quarter means that the oil market’s buffer space against supply shocks has narrowed, and oil prices will become more sensitive to geopolitical risks.

However, China's weak crude oil imports and rising demand elasticity in a high oil price environment may limit oil price increases to a certain extent. For investors looking to hedge geopolitical risks in the Middle East and Russia, Goldman Sachs recommends going long the European diesel calendar spread from December 2026 to March 2027.

The bank believes that supply in the European diesel market was already tight before the outbreak of the war, and Ukraine's continued attacks on Russian refineries also increased the risk of refined oil supply. In addition, factors such as hurricanes, extreme summer temperatures and delayed refinery maintenance may further tighten diesel supplies.

Goldman Sachs believes that compared to directly chasing crude oil prices, the European diesel spread may be a more targeted trading strategy to hedge geopolitical shocks and supply shortage risks.

UBS: Gold is still facing downward pressure in the short term and may increase holdings if it falls back to $3,850 UBS said in its latest research report that gold is currently hovering around US$4,000 per ounce, and the re-escalation of military conflicts in the Middle East and rising oil prices are posing new pressures.

Rising oil prices may push up inflation and interest rate expectations, and the market is still digesting the Fed's hawkish signal. Gold's short-term trend depends on whether investment demand can pick up. UBS pointed out that although central bank gold purchases have increased again, they are not enough to drive gold prices up alone.

It is expected that global central bank gold purchases will reach 750 to 1,000 tons in 2026, and the long-term demand for reducing holdings of US dollar assets will provide support for gold prices; however, for gold to continue to stand above $4,000, stronger investment capital inflows are still needed.

UBS estimates that quarterly investment demand needs to be close to 500 tons to significantly push up gold prices. If the U.S. economy performs better than expected, the job market tightens and oil prices continue to rise, interest rates are expected to rise further, and gold prices may fall back to $3,850 or even lower.

However, UBS believes that weak investment demand is only a temporary phenomenon, and the drop in gold prices to around $3,850 should be seen as an opportunity to increase holdings rather than a reason to turn pessimistic.

If financial market uncertainty does not lead to expectations of interest rate cuts, the boost to gold will usually be difficult to sustain. UBS predicts that as consumer spending, real wage growth and artificial intelligence-related investments gradually slow down, the Federal Reserve may cut interest rates again in March 2027.

By then, interest rate revaluation, a weakening US dollar and demand for asset diversification are expected to drive gold back to strength. The bank expects gold prices to rise to US$4,400 in September 2026, US$4,600 by the end of the year, and to US$5,000 and US$5,200 in March and June 2027, respectively. In the long term, U.S.

debt concerns and global investors' over-allocation of U.S. dollar assets still support gold's asset dispersion value.

Goldman Sachs: WAIC’s popularity of embodied intelligence surges and industrial scenarios take the lead in commercialization Goldman Sachs pointed out in its latest research report that embodied intelligence will become the most prominent theme at the 2026 World Artificial Intelligence Conference.

There were more than 1,100 exhibitors at the conference, including 242 embodied intelligence companies, a year-on-year increase of 203%, including 92 humanoid robot companies; 208 embodied intelligence products were displayed on site, a year-on-year increase of 247%, and more than 300 humanoid robots participated in the demonstration, doubling the number from the previous year.

In terms of technology, the integration of "Visual-Language-Action Model" (VLA) and "World-Action Model" (WAM) has become an industry consensus. Through the combination of fast motion control and slow cognitive reasoning, task efficiency, physical space reasoning and cross-ontology generalization capabilities can be improved.

However, the model has not yet been truly "out-of-the-box", and commercial deployment still relies on specific scenario data collection and on-site fine-tuning. Data collection is shifting from traditional teleoperation to lightweight methods such as first-person video, handheld grippers and data gloves.

Goldman Sachs predicts that the amount of global non-ontology data will expand from about 10 million hours in 2026 to hundreds of millions of hours in the next three to five years. However, the industry is still divided on the importance of data scale and high-quality information such as tactile and force control.

The progress of commercialization is obviously divided.

Logistics sorting, warehousing and handling, and some structured industrial scenarios are closest to large-scale applications; retail scenarios are still in the "available but inefficient" stage, and home scenarios are constrained by safety, cost, reliability, and non-standard environments, and are farthest away from implementation.

In terms of supply chain, wheeled chassis is still the mainstream in the near future. The number of dexterous hand manufacturers has increased from 9 to 38 in 2025. The technical routes are highly fragmented, and competition and commercialization pressure are increasing.

Goldman Sachs recommends selected targets: give Inovance Technology and Sanhua Intelligent Holdings H shares a "buy" rating; Sanhua A shares, Green Harmonic and Best are "neutral"; MOONS Electric is rated "sell" due to high valuations and intensifying competition for coreless motors and smart hands.

Deutsche Bank: Microsoft’s AI investment concerns have over-reflected that Azure’s growth rate may reach 41% Deutsche Bank said in its latest research report that the market's concerns about Microsoft's surge in capital expenditures, AI investment return rate and OpenAI order concentration have been significantly excessive, and the current valuation is attractive, maintaining a "buy" rating and a target price of $550.

Deutsche Bank estimates that Microsoft’s fourth fiscal quarter (corresponding to the second quarter of the natural year) revenue will be US$87.4 billion, a year-on-year increase of 14.3%; non-GAAP earnings per share will be US$4.17. Azure's growth rate is expected to reach 40% to 41% based on fixed exchange rates.

Enterprise cloud migration, AI computing power demand and new data center capacity will continue to support growth. Microsoft 365 commercial cloud revenue is expected to grow by 15% to 16%, and Copilot paid seats may exceed 25 million by the end of the year.

Affected by the rising prices of components such as memory and the expansion of AI infrastructure, Microsoft's fourth-quarter capital expenditure (including leasing) is expected to exceed US$40 billion, a record high; Deutsche Bank raised its capital expenditure forecast for fiscal year 2027 from US$215 billion to US$238 billion.

Microsoft's book free cash flow for the year may be close to breakeven, but it will still pay dividends of approximately US$26 billion. Deutsche Bank believes that Microsoft can alleviate profit margin pressure through AI infrastructure price increases, operating expense control and Azure revenue growth.

Regarding the concentration risk of OpenAI accounting for approximately 45% of Microsoft's backlog of orders, Deutsche Bank pointed out that this proportion is affected by the length of the long-term contract.

Among the orders to be fulfilled in the next 24 months, OpenAI may account for only 25% to 30%; even if part of the reserved capacity needs to be reallocated, it is expected to be absorbed by other customers in a market with continued shortage of computing power.

Deutsche Bank estimates that Microsoft's revenue and adjusted earnings per share in fiscal 2027 will be US$384.2 billion and US$19.45 respectively. Despite the pressure on short-term gross profit margins, Azure and Microsoft 365 can still drive the company's profits to maintain mid-double-digit growth.

Deutsche Bank raises Coca-Cola target price: growth is under pressure but full-year guidance can still be maintained Deutsche Bank said in its latest research report that despite weakening global consumer confidence, geopolitical fluctuations and increasing cost pressures, Coca-Cola is still expected to maintain mid-single-digit organic growth with its product pricing, affordability strategy, innovation capabilities and refined consumer operations.

Deutsche Bank maintained a "buy" rating and raised its target price from $88 to $92. Deutsche Bank predicts that Coca-Cola’s organic revenue will grow by 3.5% in the second quarter, with sales volume increasing by 0.9% and price and product mix contributing 2.6%; revenue is expected to be US$13.073 billion, 0.6% lower than market expectations.

In terms of regions, organic revenue in North America, Europe, the Middle East and Africa, and Latin America is expected to increase by 3%, 5%, and 6.5% respectively, while in the Asia-Pacific region, it will decrease by 1%.

Deutsche Bank expects Coca-Cola to maintain guidance for organic revenue growth of 4% to 5% and earnings per share growth of 8% to 9% for fiscal 2026. The bank forecasts full-year organic revenue growth of 4.5%, earnings before interest and taxes of US$15.949 billion, and earnings per share of US$3.24.

Fairlife recently suffered a ransomware attack and suspended production, which may drag down revenue in the third and fourth quarters, but the business is expected to recover in the first half of 2027.

Deutsche Bank believes that cost control, global procurement scale and digital investment can still support profit margins and provide a buffer for the company to maintain its full-year profit target.

They pointed out that the evolution of large models has entered the "frontier manufacturing" stage, and predicted that driven by hardware adaptation, software optimization and extreme quantification (such as ternary quantization, sub-bit quantization), the cost of AI inference will reach 10 to 50 in the next few months.

times of explosive decline, ultimately accelerating the comprehensive popularization of high-end intelligence in privatized deployment and end-side devices. Iran's Revolutionary Guards say strikes against U.S. targets in Jordan and Bahrain Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps issued multiple statements on the 21st, saying that it used missiles to attack US military and US company facilities in Jordan and Bahrain.

The statement said that the Aerospace Force of Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps used missiles to strike a U.S. military base in Jordan, destroying a missile defense radar system and destroying an F-15 fighter jet inside the bunker. The Revolutionary Guards also attacked another US military base in Jordan, killing many US soldiers.

The statement also said that the Revolutionary Guards used several cruise missiles to attack the US Amazon data center in Bahrain and destroy it in response to the US military's attack on civilian facilities under construction in Iran.

Goldman Sachs: If Hormuz is blocked for a long time, oil prices may rise above US$120 per barrel by the end of the year The war in the Middle East continues to burn, and the global oil market is standing at a historic turning point.

Goldman Sachs’ latest warning: If shipping disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz continue until 2027, Brent crude oil could break $120 as soon as the fourth quarter of this year.

Current flows in the Persian Gulf have fallen below 45% before the war, global inventories have dropped by more than 3 million barrels per day, and the market's ability to withstand shocks has narrowed sharply. Goldman Sachs recommends investors to go long European diesel spreads as the preferred tool to hedge geopolitical risks.

JPMorgan Chase CEO: Investors underestimate global risks and should not buy stocks or U.S. long-term bonds at the moment JPMorgan Chase CEO Dimon warned in a podcast that investors are underestimating the risks to the global economy and that he would not buy stocks or long-term U.S. Treasuries at current prices.

He pointed out that the market has not fully considered the increasing geopolitical and fiscal threats, and the risks are greater than the market imagines. Geopolitical conflicts between Russia, Ukraine and the Middle East, tensions between major powers, and rising military spending despite the increasing U.S.

government deficit are all the focus of his concerns. Regarding the artificial intelligence boom, Dimon pointed out that it is likely to be like the Internet, and the huge capital expenditures invested by the industry may eventually pay off, but not necessarily within the timetable expected by the market.

During the Internet era, early giants such as Yahoo eventually declined, and the final winners, Google and Facebook, emerged later. At the same time, he also said he would not buy long-term U.S. Treasury bonds. Even if inflation falls back to the Fed's 2% target level, the yield on the 10-year U.S.

Treasury note is likely to be between 4% and 4.5%, and he believes there is little room for Treasury bond prices to rise.

Tencent Cloud: Will deploy localized computing power on a large scale and deploy NPO super nodes Tencent Cloud executives recently attended a sub-forum of the World Artificial Intelligence Conference and stated that in order to reduce the cost of inference to the extreme, the company will deploy localized computing power on a large scale; the company

expects to deploy NPO (near package optics) super nodes in the fourth quarter of 2026, and called for the unification of NPO industry standards at home and abroad.

Samsung is accelerating the layout of humanoid robots: a full-time department has been formally established and research centers will be built in multiple places On Tuesday, Samsung Electronics said it would establish a robotics division "RX" to accelerate the company's R&D and commercialization process in the field of robotics and promote robots as the company's growth engine.

This department will be responsible for Samsung Electronics’ mid- to long-term robotics strategy, core technology development and business execution, while also expanding its research and development capabilities at home and abroad. Lee Dongkun, the company's executive vice president, will lead the robotics strategy team.

Lee Dongkun previously served as head of robotics strategy at Hyundai Motor Group, including leading related efforts at Boston Dynamics.

Samsung also said it plans to establish robotics research centers in areas where robotics technology is developing rapidly, such as the United States, China and Japan, to enhance its competitiveness by leveraging local ecosystems and professional talents.

The company said it plans to develop humanoid robots to improve production efficiency and user experience. It will first deploy the robots in its own factories and then expand to the retail and home fields.

TSMC is reported to increase wafer foundry prices by up to 10% starting in 2027 According to "Nikkei Asia" citing multiple sources familiar with the matter, TSMC (TSM.N) plans to increase the foundry prices of advanced and mature process chips by up to 10% in 2027 to cope with the pressure brought by rising costs of materials, manufacturing equipment and new overseas factory construction.

TSMC has started negotiations with customers on price increases, involving 7-nanometer and more advanced processes. This part of the business contributed approximately 77% of TSMC’s revenue in the April to June quarter of this year. People familiar with the matter said the base price increase ranges from 5% to 10%, depending on the customer and product.

For new orders for high-performance computing (HPC) chips that exceed customers' original forecasts, TSMC plans to charge a premium of 10% to 15% on top of the basic price increase. Therefore, the overall price increase for some advanced process chip orders may exceed 10%.

In terms of mature processes (including 12nm, 16nm, 28nm and other traditional processes), TSMC plans to increase prices by up to 10%, but the increase for some products will be lower than this level. The mature process business accounted for approximately 23% of the company's revenue in the last quarter.

People familiar with the matter said that relevant negotiations started around June and were finalized in July, and the new price will take effect in early 2027. Nvidia’s real enemy!

AMD's first rack-level AI system Helios wins big customer: Microsoft As a "trump card" to challenge Nvidia's dominance in the AI chip market, AMD is preparing to deliver its first rack-level AI system called Helios in the second half of this year. On Monday, Helios’ growing customer base once again welcomed a heavyweight company: Microsoft!

MARKET UPDATE — TUESDAY, 21 JULY 2026

Markets are trying to recover after three consecutive days of losses, but investors remain caught between optimism over the coming wave of technology earnings and growing concern that the Middle East conflict could keep inflation higher for longer.

Chip stocks are rebounding, oil remains elevated, bond yields are still near recent highs, and one of the biggest earnings weeks of the year is just beginning.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🟢 MARKETS

🔹 Dow Jones: +0.29%

European markets also moved higher.

The Euro Stoxx 50 gained approximately 1.5%, while South Korea’s KOSPI surged more than 3.5% as semiconductor shares rebounded after last week’s heavy selling.

Micron, AMD, Broadcom, Intel and Marvell all traded higher before the opening bell.

However, investors should remember what happened on Monday.

Chip shares initially rallied strongly before giving back much of their gains by the close, showing that confidence remains fragile.

Simple explanation

Investors are beginning to buy technology shares again, but they have not yet become convinced that the recent correction has finished.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🟢 BONDS & THE FED

Treasury yields remain elevated despite today’s modest moves.

🔹 2-year Treasury: 4.20%

🔹 10-year Treasury: 4.60%

🔹 30-year Treasury: 5.12%

Higher oil prices continue to keep inflation concerns alive, reducing expectations that the Federal Reserve will cut interest rates quickly.

JPMorgan CEO Jamie Dimon added to the cautious mood, saying he personally would not buy either long-dated US Treasury bonds or equities at current prices because markets appear to be underestimating geopolitical and fiscal risks.

Simple explanation

When investors worry inflation could stay high, they demand higher returns before lending money to governments.

Higher borrowing costs generally make it harder for both stocks and bonds to perform well.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🟢 UK BOND MARKET TESTS THE NEW GOVERNMENT

Britain’s new government is already facing scrutiny from financial markets.

The UK 10-year government bond yield briefly reached around 5.05%, its highest level in two months, as investors assessed Prime Minister Andy Burnham’s economic agenda and the appointment of John Healey as Chancellor.

The pressure eased slightly after data showed private-sector wage growth slowing to its weakest pace in five years, while government borrowing for June came in below expectations.

Simple explanation

Slower wage growth helps reduce inflation pressure.

However, investors also want confidence that governments will control borrowing. If they become concerned about future spending, they demand higher interest rates to lend money.

Why it matters

The UK is becoming another example of bond markets forcing governments to demonstrate fiscal discipline.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🟢 JAPAN REASSURES BOND INVESTORS

Japan approved its first major economic blueprint under Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi, emphasising investment in strategic industries while reaffirming the independence of the Bank of Japan.

The move comes after rising Japanese government bond yields unsettled investors and raised concerns that political priorities could influence monetary policy.

Simple explanation

Investors want central banks—not politicians—to decide interest rates.

If markets believe governments are encouraging artificially low interest rates while borrowing heavily, they demand higher yields to compensate for the risk.

Why it matters

Japan is one of the world’s largest investors overseas.

Higher Japanese bond yields could eventually draw money back from US and European markets.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🟢 OIL & INFLATION

Oil remains one of the market’s biggest risks.

Brent crude continues to trade close to $90 per barrel after the United States completed its tenth consecutive night of strikes against Iranian military targets.

Meanwhile:

• Vessel traffic through the Strait of Hormuz continues to decline.

• Yemen’s Houthis have declared a naval blockade targeting Saudi-linked shipping through the Bab al-Mandeb Strait.

Although mediators have proposed a temporary 10-day ceasefire, fighting continues.

Goldman Sachs has warned that Brent crude could rise above $120 per barrel later this year if significant disruption to shipping through the Strait of Hormuz persists.

Simple explanation

Around one-fifth of the world’s oil passes through the Strait of Hormuz.

If fewer ships can travel safely, global oil supplies become tighter, pushing fuel prices higher and increasing inflation.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

The recent correction in semiconductor and AI-related shares is being intensified by leverage.

Borrowing money magnifies both gains and losses.

Markets are currently balancing two competing narratives.

UBS predicts that core PCE will increase by 0.18% month-on-month and 3.34% year-on-year in June, and determines that May may be the high point of this round of inflation.

After the inflation data was released, the market's pricing for an interest rate hike at the July meeting dropped from a maximum of 11 basis points to less than 3 basis points. UBS expects the Fed to remain on hold in July and to keep interest rates unchanged for the rest of the year as core PCE slows further and employment growth weakens.

However, the market is still pricing in more than one interest rate hike before the end of the year, and expects a cumulative tightening of 42 basis points by April next year. UBS warned that geopolitics is the biggest risk to the above judgment.

After the breakdown of ceasefire negotiations between the United States and Iran, Brent crude oil rose 16% in a week to US$88 per barrel, and crude oil transportation volume in the Strait of Hormuz dropped significantly. If oil prices continue to rise, the Federal Reserve may continue to retain the option of raising interest rates.

Therefore, UBS prefers transactions that can withstand energy price risks rather than directly going long on the front-end U.S. bond duration. It is recommended to continue to hold interest rate swap receiving positions with interests starting in 3 years and a term of 4 years and long 5-year real interest rates.

In terms of the yield curve, UBS has taken profits on steepening trades in 2-year and 10-year U.S. Treasuries and turned it into neutral, believing that long-term yields have risen too fast recently. The bank expects the 2-year and 10-year U.S.

Treasury yields to be 3.95% and 4.35% respectively at the end of the year, and continues to be optimistic about long-term inflation swaps. Foreign investors bought a net $57 billion in long-term U.S. debt in May, the most since November last year, but official demand remains sluggish. Mag 7 is a thing of the past!

Citi: Betting on "Growth Clusters", covering more than half of the S&P market capitalization "Mag 7" was once a core label for measuring the performance of U.S. growth stocks, but as AI investment logic undergoes structural changes, this framework is losing its explanatory power.

Citigroup strategist Scott Chronert's team said in the latest research report that "Mag 7 is dead as a framework for assessing market growth dynamics." The team believes that investors should expand their perspective from a few technology giants to a broader "growth cluster" that covers large technology companies and most related companies that benefit from investment in AI infrastructure.

Behind this change is that the internal trend of "Mag 7" is clearly diverging. Microsoft and Meta are under pressure as the market re-examines the return cycle of huge AI capital expenditures. Apple is favored by funds because of its more restrained data center investment strategy, with growth reaching 23% during the year.

At the same time, the overall performance of the Bloomberg "Mag 7" index has lagged behind the broader market this year, and the traditional logic of grouping is loosening.

In fact, Chronert predicted as early as December last year that the AI trading logic in 2026 will shift from "AI infrastructure providers" to "AI technology adopters", and the current market rotation is gradually verifying this judgment.

Funds are migrating from pure hardware suppliers to application layer companies that can use AI to improve efficiency and improve products. This structural change is more profound than simple sector rotation.

Chip stocks fell into a bear market, JPMorgan Chase called out "summer bargain hunting", but Morgan Stanley continued to bet on cloud giants Amid the deep correction in chip stocks, the two giants on Wall Street have clear differences in their judgments on the market outlook - JPMorgan Chase believes that the time to buy is approaching, while Morgan Stanley insists that hyperscalers are more attractive and has reservations about whether chip stocks can regain market leadership.

The Philadelphia Semiconductor Index (SOX) fell 10% in a single week last week, marking its worst weekly performance since April 2025. As a result, it has fallen more than 20% from the historical closing high set a month ago, officially entering a technical bear market. In this context, the J.P.

Morgan strategist team believes that chip stocks "will soon begin to receive buying support" and recommends that investors choose opportunities to rearrange their positions this summer; the Morgan Stanley strategist team predicts that even if chip stocks rebound, it will be difficult to regain their leading position in the second half of this year, and they are more inclined to hold ultra-large cloud computing companies.

Regarding the market trend, Morgan Stanley maintains the S&P 500 index's year-end target of 8,000 points, believing that this target is still possible to achieve.

The team said that the S&P 500's shock consolidation in the past two months was in line with previous expectations, and the market structure was shifting from being driven by a few technology leaders to broader industry participation.

However, Morgan Stanley also warned that if the momentum trading unwinding spreads further or the situation in the Middle East continues to escalate, the S&P 500 may face further adjustment pressure and seek support near 7,000 points. This point is regarded as an important technical support area before the bull market trend restarts.

Morgan Stanley "leads" Wall Street in AI debt trading: earning $2.3 billion in revenue in half a year Morgan Stanley has become Wall Street’s main promoter of building an artificial intelligence financing structure.

The bank continues to devise new debt and equity models to channel tens of billions of dollars into the construction of large-scale data centers. The bank has become a leading financing advisor, leading the largest and most innovative AI infrastructure financing projects since last year, according to industry executives.

These include the exclusive underwriting of a $3.2 billion bond for data center developer TeraWulf, Meta's $27 billion debt financing program in partnership with Blue Owl to build Hyperion data centers, and most recently advising Broadcom on a $35 billion chip financing deal.

The bank's debt and equity capital markets fees surged in the first half to $2.3 billion, up from $1.4 billion in the same period last year, according to LSEG. The performance allowed it to overtake long-time rival Goldman Sachs Group Inc. and move Morgan Stanley into second place behind JPMorgan Chase & Co.

in global capital markets revenue rankings, up from fourth the year before. These deals show that artificial intelligence is reshaping not only the technology sector but also capital markets.

Rather than relying solely on traditional project finance or corporate lending, bankers are increasingly devising new structural models that package long-term computing contracts and the balance sheets of big tech companies into products that can be sold to mainstream investors.

CPU returns to the main stage of AI, Intel’s Q2 financial report faces the most critical battle Intel is at a critical turning point.

As the AI workload structure shifts from training to inference and then to agents, the strategic position of CPUs in data centers has quietly rebounded, and the investment logic of this veteran chip giant is being repriced by the market. Intel will announce its second-quarter earnings report after the U.S. stock market closes on July 23.

This is not an ordinary quarterly performance disclosure - with the stock price rising by more than 160% during the year and the valuation still being at a high level of about 87 times the expected price-earnings ratio, Wall Street requires management to simultaneously deliver on revenue growth, gross profit margin improvement and sustainable growth

guidance, and provide tangible evidence to prove that data center CPU demand and foundry business transformation have entered a quantifiable realization stage. Most analysts are betting that this performance will exceed the company's original guidance, and HSBC raised its target price by 100% to US$200, the highest target among Wall Street analysts.

However, the market's high expectations themselves pose risks.

After Intel announced a better-than-expected outlook for the first quarter, it rose 19% in after-hours trading, and then rose sharply driven by the AI chip boom and expectations of local manufacturing policies; but as of July 17, the stock price had retreated sharply from its June high to around $95, and the Philadelphia Semiconductor Index fell more than 20% from its high during the same period, entering a technical bear market.

Whether the financial report can serve as a catalyst for a new round of upside, or whether it will turn into a "good news", will largely depend on Intel's ability to prove its ability to translate AI demand into deliverable capacity and sustainable profits. Boeing's production capacity expansion reaches a critical turning point!

FAA resumes final release authority for new 737 Max and 787 aircraft After a near-catastrophic crash involving a 737 Max aircraft in 2024, regulators found a series of quality issues at Boeing. Subsequently, the U.S.

Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) restricted Boeing's aircraft production and revoked the company's authority to perform final release signatures on new production aircraft.

The FAA said the decision to restore Boeing's relevant authority will officially take effect on July 20, which reflects the company's progress in improving quality management and regaining the trust of regulators. This is crucial for Boeing to expand production, improve its financial position and regain its advantage against rival Airbus.

Currently, the American aircraft manufacturer is working hard to stabilize production of its "cash cow" 737 series aircraft, aiming to maintain monthly production at 47 aircraft, with plans to gradually increase to 63 aircraft in the next few years. In addition, the FAA said regulators will continue to monitor the aircraft manufacturer's quality levels.

Goldman Sachs is telling clients oil could blow past 110 dollars a barrel as the US and Israeli war on Iran chokes Gulf shipping again, and the bank is pricing that possibility like a weather forecast.

Gulf exports had recovered to more than 80 percent of pre-war levels after Washington and Tehran signed a memorandum of understanding in June, but renewed tanker attacks in the Strait of Hormuz have pushed flows below 50 percent, around 11 million barrels a day. Goldman estimates the market is short 13.4 million barrels a day of Persian Gulf supply. The bank holds its Brent forecast at 80 dollars for the fourth quarter of 2026 and 75 for 2027, while warning prices could overshoot 110 if recovery stalls, or drop into the 60s if production rebounds. A reinstated US blockade of Iranian ports could strip another 1.5 to 2 million barrels a day from Iran's exports.

This crisis exists because the United States and Israel launched an air war on Iran in late February 2026, after which Iran's Revolutionary Guard mined the strait and struck merchant tankers in retaliation. A June memorandum briefly reopened flows before fighting resumed. None of that appears in the market note. What appears instead is a blockade of a sovereign nation's ports rendered as a probability input, stripped of the fact that a blockade is itself an act of war.

Who cashes in on the chokepoint: Goldman's trading desks profit from movement, and a 50 dollar spread between downside and upside scenarios is itself a tradable asset. Gulf monarchies hosting US military infrastructure reroute crude through the Red Sea and collect a scarcity premium. Iranian civilians and stranded tanker crews absorb the actual cost of a war fought to police who sells energy from the Gulf.

My take: Wall Street has turned a war on Iran into a forecasting exercise with no casualties in the model. Goldman's note treats mined shipping lanes and blockaded ports as probability inputs, never as the outcome of a US and Israeli decision to bomb Iran in February. The bank's numbers—an 80 point swing in export flows, a 13.4 million barrel shortfall, a 50 dollar price band—describe a war economy dressed as a supply chain problem. Every dollar Brent climbs is a transfer from ordinary drivers and Iranian civilians to institutions structuring bets on both directions at once.

Chips bounced — AMD launched Helios and Alphabet confirmed a new AI chip — but broader risk appetite stayed muted as the US-Iran situation worsened over the weekend.

Two things defined today. First, oil touched $90 a barrel overnight after the US conducted another round of airstrikes against Iran and confirmed the death of another American service member. Crude pulled back throughout the day, settling near $81, but the supply picture is getting harder to ignore: US crude inventories are at 43 days of supply, the lowest level in 45 years. 

Second, the market is holding its breath. This is the most important earnings week of the summer. GM reports tomorrow morning, Alphabet and Tesla report Wednesday after the close, Intel reports Thursday. These aren't just individual stock events — they're the real test of whether AI spending is producing returns or whether cost-of-AI anxiety spreads from chips into the hyperscalers. Last week established the pattern: beats get sold when capex guidance rises. This week we find out if that logic holds for the companies actually writing the checks.

The Dow's 0.59% decline dragged the financials lower in sympathy, but nothing about today's session changes the thesis. The fee engine that just printed a four-for-four earnings sweep doesn't reset because of a single mixed Monday. If anything, the growing IPO pipeline keeps validating the story — Jersey Mike's ($JMKE) launched its roadshow today, targeting ~$1B with Morgan Stanley and JPMorgan as global coordinators and Goldman in the syndicate. SpaceX, Anthropic, SK Hynix, now Jersey Mike's — every major listing this year runs through the same desks. The advisory revenue pipeline is visible through the back half of 2026.

Stock of the Day: $PLTR. Palantir closed at $134.62, up 1.69% on a day when the Dow fell nearly 300 points and the S&P closed red. That's the kind of relative strength worth flagging. The chart tells the story — PLTR gapped down at the open, reversed hard through midday, and held its gains into the close while the broader tape faded. When Iran escalation is the headline and AI spending is the question of the week, Palantir sits at the intersection of both — defense contracts that benefit from geopolitical tension, and an AI platform that doesn't carry the capex anxiety dragging down the chip names.

Three things to watch tomorrow: GM earnings before the bell (the first read on whether the consumer economy is holding), oil direction as the Iran strikes continue, and positioning ahead of Wednesday's Alphabet/Tesla double-header. If Alphabet's AI capex commentary echoes TSMC's pattern from last week — good numbers punished on rising costs — tech could get volatile. If the results clear the bar, the risk-off tone of the last five sessions has room to reverse.

2026年7月20日盘后分析

美股进入关键转折周：宏观风险叠加财报考研，机构策略重心正在转向

2026年7月20日，美股步入了一个至关重要的时间节点。本周不仅面临美联储政策动向的持续影响，更有包括谷歌（Google）和特斯拉（Tesla）在内的多项科技巨头财报密集出炉。这些财报不仅将决定个股走势，更将考验目前相对脆弱的AI板块，尤其是AI硬件领域的韧性。

一、 宏观警报：地缘局势推动通胀风险重燃

目前市场最真金白银的风险信号来自于霍尔木兹海峡。受地缘冲突升级影响，航运保险公司已将该地区的战争险保费率推升至8%至10%，这达到了本轮冲突爆发以来的最高水平。

这种局势的恶化直接导致原油价格一度逼近90美元，并存在重回100美元大关的可能。对于美联储而言，这无疑是“致命打击”，可能引发加息预期的再度升温，从而使处于高位震荡的股指面临下行回落的巨大风险。

二、 机构策略转向：“重个股、轻大盘”的分散度交易

高盛最新的报告指出，目前期权市场的隐含相关性已接近20年来的最低水平。这预示着一个重大转变：分散度交易（Dispersion Trading）将成为年内机构的核心焦点。

这意味着市场走势将更多受个股微观因素驱动，而非宏观大环境。板块和个股之间的分化会愈发剧烈，选股逻辑将比赛道选择更加挑剔。在这一背景下，投资者应优先选择基本面强劲、现金流充足、且估值与技术面仍具上行空间的标的，并对高估值或技术面破位的风险个股及时锁定获利。

三、 AI板块内部分化：资金在轮转而非撤离

尽管AI芯片板块近期波动剧烈，但市场并未出现全面溃败。资金展现出了明显的内部轮转迹象：
资金流向： 从通用AI芯片向定制化芯片（如AVGO、Marvel）、内存芯片及上游设备商转移。
关键博弈： 市场正在屏息以待下周**微软（Microsoft）**的财报，特别是其对AI大规模资本支出合理性的解释，这将是决定AI投资情绪能否延续的关键。

四、 重点个股实战观察

ServiceNow (NOW)： 技术面表现稳健，已成功重回50日和100日均线上方，显示出强劲的反弹动能。期权市场对其财报持明显看涨态度，且其在软件股中具备较好的基本面前景，是目前相对安全的配置目标。
谷歌 (Google/GOOG)： 350美元是关键支撑位和高性价比切入点。目前股价处于低位反弹蓄势阶段，虽然长期基本面无虞，但期权市场情绪偏空，仓位过重者（超过40-50%）需警惕财报后的剧烈震荡。
美国运通 (AXP)： 尽管技术面在高位出现震荡回落信号，但期权市场对其未来一个月的走势极度看涨。由于其服务于高端客户，具备较强的抗风险能力，建议持有者等待财报表现。
防御性板块： 在现价角度，Costco (COST) 比刚破位的沃尔玛 (WMT) 更具吸引力，其底部反弹信号已初现，适合作为避险配置。

五、 总结与展望

当前大盘虽有偏空预期，但尚未出现恐慌性挤兑。板块赛道的持续轮转（如资金从AI硬件转向软件，或在芯片内部切换）起到了稳定器的作用。

Iran Strikes Bahrain, Kuwait and Jordan as Ceasefire Proposal Surfaces

Iran struck US bases in Bahrain, Kuwait, and Jordan with missiles and drones on July 21, 2026, in response to ten consecutive nights of American bombardments on Iranian territory. Bahrain's air forces shot down a wave of incoming Iranian missiles; air raid sirens sounded at least four times in Manama. The United States conducted nighttime strikes in southern Iran after President Trump declared that Tehran would pay for the deaths of American soldiers. Overnight, US forces also used unmanned surface drones to destroy an Iranian submarine in an operation carried out on July 13, documented by footage released by CENTCOM. Two oil tankers were struck after traveling unauthorized routes through the Strait of Hormuz as designated by Tehran.

SCENARIO
The conflict is likely to continue at a gradual escalation pace for several days, with overnight exchange of strikes between US and Iranian forces and Iranian strikes on US regional bases. A 10-day ceasefire proposal is on the table, but rapid acceptance is unlikely: IRGC factions and Iranian conservative elements remain opposed to any deal perceived as capitulation, and Washington has not publicly signaled a pause in operations. The Strait of Hormuz is likely to remain partially operational but under persistent threat, with LNG supplies to Asia and southern Europe likely to remain disrupted.

🚨 This is no longer a single act of retaliation. It is a systematic effort to disarm Iran. Every new night of strikes may weaken its arsenal — while moving the region closer to a war whose costs could be felt far beyond the Middle East.

For anyone not following closely: U.S. forces are conducting a sustained campaign that Washington says is intended to reduce Tehran’s ability to attack American forces and commercial shipping in the region.

During the night of July 20–21, in the tenth consecutive night of operations, forces under @CENTCOM struck Iranian military command centers, missile and drone launch sites, air-defense systems and maritime capabilities. The U.S. command said the objective was to degrade Iran’s ability to keep attacking vessels moving through the Strait of Hormuz.

That is where this becomes a problem for ordinary people. Hormuz is a vital global energy route. An attack on a tanker, a disrupted shipping lane or another round of retaliation can raise oil, fuel, freight and insurance costs — and eventually make everyday goods more expensive.

Militarily, Washington is trying to hit the opponent’s entire “nervous system”: command structures, launchers, radars and air defenses. Politically, the danger is greater. The more effective the strikes appear, the stronger the pressure on Tehran to retaliate and prove it has not been paralyzed.

This conflict shows how global economic security can depend on a handful of bases, radar sites and one narrow waterway. The question is no longer whether the latest targets were destroyed. It is whether these strikes will force Iran toward an accord — or trigger retaliation that neither side can control.

Sources: @CENTCOM, @Reuters, @AP

US launches fresh strikes on Iran, as Trump warns of retaliation for deaths of soldiers

Jaroslav Lukiv

The US military says it launched another round of strikes against Iran to "further degrade" its capabilities to attack ships in the Strait of Hormuz.

The strikes - the 10th consecutive night of attacks - come after US President Donald Trump vowed to punish Iran for the deaths of US soldiers.

Trump earlier said Iran had been hit "very hard" the previous night "in honour" of three US soldiers killed recently - two in Jordan and one in Iraq.

In response to the latest wave, Iran said it struck two oil tankers in the waterway, as well as US military assets in Bahrain and Kuwait, state news agency IRNA reported.

President Masoud Pezeshkian said Iran was now fighting a "full-scale war" with the US.

At the same time, Tehran said diplomatic messages were still being exchanged by both sides through intermediaries, despite a collapse of a ceasefire.

US Central Command (Centcom) said on X that the latest strikes had hit Iranian "military command centres, maritime capabilities, missile and drone launch sites, and air defense systems".

Commercial vessels have continued to pass through the Strait of Hormuz, Centcom said.

Iran said rescue teams were removing the crews from the two oil tankers it had struck while attempting to transit through the vital waterway, according to the state news agency IRNA.

The United Kingdom Maritime Trade Operations agency (UKMTO) said a tanker in the Strait of Hormuz had reported being struck by an unknown projectile, forcing its crew to abandon the vessel and board a lifeboat.

It was not clear whether the tanker reported by UKMTO was among those cited by Iran.

On Tuesday night the Department of Defense confirmed the identities of two of the US soldiers – Pte Isabella Gonzales, 19, from Texas, and Lt Tyler James Feehan, 25, from Hawaii - who were killed in an Iranian strike on the Muwaffaq Salti base in Jordan over the weekend.

Centcom said earlier a third service member also went missing in action after the attack, and unidentified remains were later found in the area.

Elsewhere in the region, Jordan's military said late on Monday it had shot down three Iranian missiles as sirens sounded across the country warning of incoming fire.

Earlier in the day, Kuwait's armed forces said they had intercepted Iranian drones.

Bahrain said drones launched by Iran had targeted its air navigation systems - but traffic remained unaffected.

On the US strikes the previous night, Iran's semi-official Tasnim news agency reported that several cities had been hit including Tabriz, Chabahar, Konarak, Bandar Mahshahr and Bandar Imam Khomeini.

Iranian state media reported that two oil tankers had exploded as the vessels tried to cross the strait.

Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) said the tankers were going through what it called an "unsafe and hazardous southern route" of the strait and "exploded and were brought to a halt". Centcom disputed the claims.

CRUDE OIL - WHAT IS NEXT?

I closed 75-80% of my Brent longs (long futures / sold puts / long UZ and VZ spreads) last week and today. Still keeping a decent amount of oil stocks (half - in smaller names, fairly levered to next 2 years prices, but with little or no debt).

Will probably get yelled at again by fellow oil investors :) - like it happened at the end of April.

Anyway, I was buying dips created by "deal is imminent" announcements by Trump & Co since March 9th, reducing risk in $100-120 area and it worked very well, except for the last time (MOU). I started adding way to early in June and had a larger than expected drawdown. While I ultimately made almost all the money back and overall am up quite a lot since the start of the war, this is not meant to be a victory lap - rather the review of what I got wrong.

The "long oil" trade always had two components:

1) geopolitical (Iran's leverage is proportionate to the tightness of the global oil market & maxed before US midterms / they also made no secret of the plan to have some degree of control over Hormuz). This part worked. I was surprised that the market started to price full recovery right after MOU despite vague language (that actually favoured Iran) and lack of real agreement on pretty much any substantial issue (nuclear / Hormuz). Felt wrong.

In reality it turned out that despite clause 5 of the MOU being much closer to "Iran controls the Strait" than to proper freedom of navigation, US had no intention to cede Hormuz (and the scale of US response was harsher than I anticipated). 

Geopolitical logic favouring long trade (at least from today's 87-91 levels) probably still stands, but I do not feel that I have any edge there. It seems that full normalisation is unlikely for quite a while, but there are tons of scenarios other than the escalation trap. The market could stay in limbo for some time - with partial, but still substantial flows or - eventually - after false starts and episodes of hostilities - come some type of arrangement that gives Iran partial, but not full control (joint management with Oman or other Gulf countries, perhaps in exchange to released funds/sanction relief etc). 

It is fairly clear that despite larger than expected escalation the parties are not targeting energy & export infra aggressively - there were some hits, but they were limited. So while hitting a port or a tanker is probably easier than attacking a US base, Iran has shown certain restraint there - which makes me think that the logic by which Iran will end up with full control because they *could* create a very large problem for oil production/exports does not necessarily work in a straightforward way.

I am not an expert there, so do not want to spend much time on that.

2) fundamental angle. Basically my original idea was that the scale of production loss is so large that the crude prices should stay relatively high at least till year end - and trades with breakeven in mid 80s seemed like a no brainer.

While I do not think now that it was/is necessarily wrong, the fundamental case has weakened.

Lots was said about China imports and flexibility of global refining system (managing to concentrate most of run cuts in petchem). There are a couple more factors that I underestimated:

a) Scale of refining bottleneck. There are 3 components there
- RU 2mbd+ refining losses due to drone attacks
- China. It looks like they are able to manage 2.5mbd refinery run cuts with just 600kbd drop in transport fuel production (which in itself reflects substitution). This is probably not sustainable long term as the petchem part of run cuts in part represents final product destocking, but I honestly do not think they will come aggressively bidding for crude at $90+. They also had around 1.9bln barrels between crude and products inventory (as per GS) before the war so could ease some run cuts by drawing  inventory more heavily

covered it here
https://t.co/HEi3hPznYM

- MEG refiners: it looks like product exports recovery could be lagging crude exports recovery - even desite crazy refining margins (see (b))

Add these 3 and it is easy to see how we ended up with record cracks, very high product prices (approaching demand destruction levels) and meh crude. I certainly underestimated the impact and e.g. closed ICE Gasoil spreads way too early leaving 3/4 of the money on the table.

b) Crude export recovery. It seems to me that the pre-MOU assumptions of the  about crude exports recovery were too conservative. 

"Hormuz shuttle" scheme - when risk-taking NOC (UAE mainly), Sinokor and greek tankers shuttle through Hormuz and S2S to more risk averse owners outside of the Strait really makes a difference here. At the same time there is no similar scheme for products - so product exports and MEG refinery outage recovery would be lagging. And the scheme and high risk environment raise freight costs and hurt premiums  but also benchmark crude prices indirectly (remember that China ultimately cares about landed price).

This again ties to refining bottleneck - if we go back to end of June environment with high refinery outages in China / RU /MEG and relatively large crude outflows from the Gulf we could have moderate crude prices even despite still large production outages, very high products and crazy cracks.

Anyway even though the fundamental bull case is certainly not dead (we do have a large inventory draw - EA estimated it at 650mb across products and crude at the end of June - incl. unobservable; I imagine it should be at least 700-750mb by end of July?;  I am sure that a large part of demand reduction is postponed demand, so it will come back), the trade now leans much stronger on geopolitics.

I do not have an angle there + having a very large long position that makes money on escalation screws with your incentives. To be clear - I do not think there is anything morally wrong with being long or short oil, but I really hate war and would rather sleep better when things are more peaceful ....

CHINA DEMAND

IEA report estimates that China demand dropped 1.6mbd YoY in May. Of thouse gasoline is down only 170kbd (but it was projected to drop 120kbd in the baseline pre-war 2026 demand forecast), diesel @ -330kbd vs projected growth of 45pbd. Jet was down 100kbd vs 46kbd projected growth.

Pre-war they had 2025 and 2026 demand growth of roughly 200kbd, so this means 1.8mbd from baseline.

With crude imports drop around 4mbd (vs average of 2025) and refinerly run cuts of 2.5mbd this implies circa 1.5-2mbd between pause in crude stockpiling (1mbd?) and crude draws (another 0.5mbd).  And another 0.7mbd product draws.

I am assuming above that local production growth YoY roughly matches the pre-war baseline demand growth (in reality it was slightly smaller, but within 100kbd).

Then part of the 1.8mbd demand drop - which is mainly concentrated in petchem - is a mix of pause in petchem product stockpiling and petchem products stock draws; ultimately in steady state at least petchem stock draws should reverse.

Overall, it seems to me that Chinese demand drop is much less dramatic, then is often portrayed - most of the adjustment were indeed stockpiling pause and stock draws, but since they were spread between visible and invisible crude storage, product storage (less observable) and petchem product storage (methodologically does not feed oil stock draws, as those are 1 step down the production chain) each component was either not that big or not visible.

Strait of Hormuz and Global Oil Prices
Market report — July 20, 2026

Executive summary
The Strait of Hormuz is presently the dominant geopolitical risk in the oil market. Renewed U.S.–Iran fighting, attacks on commercial vessels, and sharply reduced tanker traffic have pushed Brent crude back above $90 per barrel, up from below $70 following June’s interim ceasefire. The current price therefore contains both a genuine physical-supply shortage and a substantial security premium. Axios, 

Associated Press
The disruption matters disproportionately because Hormuz normally carries roughly one-fifth of global petroleum supply. Alternative pipelines can bypass only a fraction of this volume, so even partial obstruction rapidly affects freight, insurance, inventories, refinery margins, inflation, and consumer fuel prices worldwide.

Why Hormuz matters
The strait is the only maritime exit from the Persian Gulf. Before the present crisis, it handled:
About 20–23 million barrels per day
Roughly 20% of global petroleum consumption
Approximately 29% of worldwide seaborne oil flows
Major exports from Saudi Arabia, Iraq, the UAE, Kuwait, Qatar and Iran

Asia is especially exposed because most Hormuz cargoes travel to China, India, Japan, South Korea and Southeast Asia. Europe is affected through global benchmark pricing and competition for Atlantic Basin barrels; North America is less physically dependent but still pays globally influenced crude and fuel prices. U.S. EIA chokepoint analysis
Saudi and Emirati pipelines can divert some production to the Red Sea and Fujairah, but the EIA estimates only around 2.6 million barrels per day of effective unused bypass capacity—far below normal Hormuz traffic. EIA country analysis

What has happened to prices
The market has experienced several distinct phases:
Initial closure shock: After military action and the de facto closure in early March, tanker movements and Gulf production fell sharply. Brent briefly approached $120 per barrel. IEA March report

Persistent physical disruption: Reduced exports caused inventories to fall, regional production to be shut in, and oil to accumulate in locations unable to ship it. On-land stocks dropped by approximately 170 million barrels in April. IEA May report

Ceasefire relief: June’s interim agreement raised expectations of reopening, bringing Brent below $70.

Renewed escalation: The agreement deteriorated, attacks and blockades resumed, and Brent climbed from approximately $86.57 on July 14 to above $90 on July 19–20. Axios bypass analysis, Axios market update

Volatility has also increased dramatically. The EIA calculates that Brent’s average daily price movement during April and May was about $4 per barrel, compared with $1 during the same period in 2025. EIA petroleum-market assessment

How the shock spreads globally
Physical supply: Tankers cannot safely or economically carry normal volumes. Exporting states eventually reduce production when storage fills, turning a transportation disruption into lost global output.

Freight and insurance: War-risk premiums and scarce willing tanker capacity raise the delivered price even for cargoes that successfully transit. Middle East-to-Asia VLCC rates reached their highest level in EIA records dating to 2005. EIA tanker-rate analysis

Refined products: Diesel, jet fuel and petrochemical feedstocks are particularly exposed. Refiners outside the Gulf must bid for replacement crude, while exported Gulf products become harder to obtain.

Inflation and growth: Higher transportation, electricity, fertilizer and manufacturing costs pass through to consumer prices. Import-dependent emerging economies face the additional burden of weaker currencies and larger trade deficits.

Regional differences: Asian buyers bear the greatest direct replacement cost. Europe competes for West African, North Sea and U.S. barrels. American production cushions the United States, but does not isolate its gasoline and diesel markets from global pricing.

Outlook
ScenarioLikely market directionSecure reopening with sustained tanker trafficRapid removal of the security premium; Brent could fall materially, although depleted inventories would slow normalizationLimited escorted passages and intermittent attacksElevated, highly volatile prices around current levelsProlonged severe restrictionRenewed pressure toward the March highs as inventories fall and Gulf production is shut inDamage to export terminals, pipelines or processing facilitiesPotentially more severe and longer-lasting spike than a shipping-only disruption
